scale_meaning: This scale is descriptive, not scored. Unlike the composite index (data/composite/), these answers are not blended into a single number — several questions here are genuinely double-edged (a digital ID can be citizen-empowering or surveillance-enabling depending on its architecture) and collapsing them into one score would hide that judgment call rather than make it. Read the note field on each answer; it carries the actual content.

questions.text: Is there a digital ID in place?
questions.guidance: Yes = a national digital identity system exists and is in active, mandatory-or-de-facto-required use for accessing government or financial services (e.g. Aadhaar, Estonia's e-ID). Partial = exists but optional, limited rollout, or state/regional rather than national. No = no national digital ID system. The note must state whether the architecture is centralized (state-held database) or decentralized/citizen-controlled (e.g. cryptographic ID the citizen holds) — this distinction matters more than yes/no.

questions.text: Is it (still) possible to pay in cash?
questions.guidance: Yes = cash is legal tender and widely accepted for everyday transactions with no significant barriers. Partial = legal but usage is declining, or accepted with limits (transaction caps, many merchants cashless by choice or law). No = cash effectively phased out or heavily restricted by law.

Compulsory school attendance up to a certain age is nearly universal, so yes/no alone carries little signal. The differentiator to capture in the note is whether homeschooling / alternative education is legally permitted: Yes = compulsory schooling AND homeschooling banned or heavily restricted. Partial = compulsory schooling, homeschooling legal with regulation. No = no compulsory schooling requirement.

questions.text: Is there a social credit system?
questions.guidance: Yes = an active, government-operated system that scores or rates citizens' trustworthiness/compliance with real consequences (blacklisting, restrictions) tied to the score. Partial = fragmented or sectoral blacklist systems (e.g. courts blacklisting debt defaulters) without a unified citizen score. No = no such system. This term is widely mythologized in Western coverage — the note must distinguish what is actually documented from popular characterization.

questions.text: Can I trade in alternative currencies like crypto, gold etc.?
questions.guidance: Yes = legal to buy, hold, and trade cryptocurrency and precious metals, largely unrestricted. Partial = legal but heavily regulated (e.g. banned as payment but allowed as a held asset, or requires licensing). No = banned.

Yes = broad mandatory insurance requirements (e.g. mandatory health insurance for all residents). Partial = mandatory only in narrow, specific cases (e.g. vehicle liability insurance only). No = no mandatory insurance requirements.

Yes = significant government-imposed restrictions on citizens' domestic or international movement in practice (exit visas, internal permit systems, politically-motivated passport denial). Partial = restrictions apply to specific groups or regions only. No = free movement domestically and internationally.

questions.text: Are there restrictions of internet access?
questions.guidance: Yes = extensive government filtering or blocking of online content/platforms. Partial = selective blocking of specific sites or services. No = open access.

Yes = political speech, government criticism, and independent press are legally protected and practically tolerated. Partial = legal protection exists but with significant practical limits or chilling effects. No = criticism of the government is criminalized or suppressed in practice.
